Facts.text: Pactum Sicardi is signed, a peace agreement between the Principality of Benevento and the Duchy of Naples
Saint Ulrich of Augsburg is canonized
Brightest known supernova SN 1054 (creates the Crab Nebula) 1st reported by Chinese astronomers
Jordan II of Capua is anointed as prince after his infant nephew's death
Battle at Breukelen: Holland vs Lichtenberg
Francesco II Ordelaffi of Forlì surrenders to the Papal commander Gil de Albornoz.
Angelo Correr renounces his claim to the Papacy as Pope Gregory XII
41 Jewish martyrs burned at stake at Breslau
The city of Trois-Rivières is founded in New France, later to become the Canadian province of Quebec
City of Providence, Rhode Island form
Prince of Condé starts blood bath in Paris
English Barebones Parliament goes into session
Battle at Boussu-lez-Walcourt: French-English vs Dutch army
Orangetown Resolutions adopted in the Province of New York, one of many protests against the British Parliament's Coercive Acts
French fleet occupies Grenada
1st US tariff act signed by President Washington
1st Independence Day celebration is held
1st US Military Academy opens at West Point, NY
French troops occupy Amsterdam
Chief Engineer James Geddes begins construction on the Erie Canal, (Rome, New York), one of the first great engineering works in North America
Slavery abolished in New York
Construction begins on B & O (Baltimore-Ohio) 1st US passenger RR
Cornerstone is laid for the second US Mint at Chestnut and Juniper Streets, Philadelphia, the "Grecian Temple"
The first London bus "omnibus," operated by George Shillibeer, begins service between Marylebone Road and Bank Junction
"America (My Country 'Tis of Thee)" with lyrics by Samuel Francis Smith has its first public performance at Park Street Church in Boston, Massachusetts
Wisconsin Territory forms
Grand Junction Railway, the world's first long-distance railway, opens between Birmingham and Liverpool
Huskar Pit Disaster: 26 children drown while trying to escape flooding in the Silkstone Colliery in England. Leads to the 1842 Mines Act, which bans women and children from working underground.
Iowa Territory is organised from Wisconsin Territory, lasting until 1846
The Cunard Line's 700-ton wooden paddle steamer RMS Britannia departs from Liverpool bound for Halifax, Nova Scotia, on the first transatlantic crossing with a scheduled end
Texas Congress votes for annexation to US
In a special session of 27th Congress Lincoln requests 400,000 troops
Skirmish at Harper's Ferry, West Virginia
R Morgan's: Tomkinsville, KY to Somerset, KY [->JUL 28]
Boise, Idaho, founded (now capital of Idaho)
Failed Confederate assault on Helena Arkansas (640 casualties)
Skirmish at Smithburg, Tennessee
Vicksburg, Mississippi surrenders to Union forces
First edition of "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland" byLewis Carrollis published
Firecracker thrown in wood starts fire destroying half of Portland, Maine, US
The Republican Party of Texas is founded in Houston, with a goal regaining admittance to the union after the US Civil War
Battle at Ueno, Japan: last Tokugawa armies defeated
Māori leader Te Kooti and 300 of his followers capture the schooner Rifleman in the Chatham Islands and sail to New Zealand, landing at Whareongaonga six days later
Aquarium opens in Woodward Gardens, San Francisco
Social Democratic Workmen's Party of North America formed
White Democrats kill several blacks in terrorist attacks in Vicksburg
1st public exhibition of electric light in San Francisco
Batholdi visits Bedloe Island, future home of his Statue of Liberty
Africaner Union forms by Rev SJ du Toit at Cape colony
Battle at Ulundi: Lord Chelmsford beats Zulu King Cetshwayo
Telegraph Hill Observatory opens in San Francisco
First US bullfight held in Dodge City, KS
First scheduled transcontinental passenger train reaches Port Moody, British Columbia
First organized rodeo competition held in Prescott, Arizona
Washington state constitutional convention holds 1st meeting
James Keir Hardie chosen 1st socialist in British Lower house
Western Samoa changes the International Date Line, resulting in 367 days in the country that year with two occurrences of Monday, July 4
Elwood Haynes successfully tests one of 1st US autos at 6 MPH
Republic of Hawaii proclaimed, Sanford B Dole as president
Katherine Lee Bates publishes "America the Beautiful"
French liner "La Bourgogne" collides with bark Cromartyshire, 560 die
US flag hoisted over Wake Island (Spanish–American War)
Williams Jennings Bryan nominated as Democratic Party presidential candidate, USA
Pacific Cable (San Francisco, Hawaii, Guam, Philippines) opens, PresidentTheodore Rooseveltsends a message
Great Britain, France, and Italy declare independence of Ethiopia (Abyssinia), but all lay claim to their own 'spheres of influence' in that land
MLB New York Giants pitcher George "Hooks" Wiltse no-hits Philadelphia Phillies, 1-0 in 10 inning; missed a perfect game by hitting one batter with a pitch (Polo Grounds, NYC)
British Secret Service Bureau forms foreign section, the Secret Intelligence Service (SIS), commonly known as MI6
In a mutual defense agreements, Japan and Russia delineate their spheres of interest in Manchuria
The US Congress pass the Mann-Elkins Act, an important piece of railroad reform legislation
105°F (41°C) at Vernon, Vermont (state record)
106°F (41°C) at Nashua, New Hampshire (state record)
Detroit Tiger George Mullen no-hits St Louis Browns, 7-0
Jack Johnsonbeats "Fireman" Jim Flynn by disqualification in 9 in Las Vegas to retain world heavyweight boxing title
Altar dedicated at full-scale replica of Stonehenge at Maryhill, Washington
ADGB (Allgemeine Deutsche Gewerkschaftsbund) party forms
The provisional government of Siberia's Maritime Province agrees to hand over parts of the strategic oil- and coal-rich Sakhalin Islands to Japan
Jack Dempseybeats Tommy Gibbons on points over 15 hard fought rounds in Shelby, Montana to retain world heavyweight boxing title
Italian immigrant chef Caesar Cardini, creates his famous salad for the very first time, at his restaurant in Tijuana, Mexico
MLB New York Yankees Herb Pennock beats Philadelphia A's Lefty Grove 1-0 in 15 innings in first game of a doubleheader at Yankee Stadium
The former Dreyfus Hotel collapses in Boston, Massachusetts, killing 44 of an estimated 200 patrons dancing in the second floor Pickwick Club
NSDAP-party forms in Weimar
First flight of the Lockheed Vega, a pilot plus four-passenger monoplane; its 225 HP engine allowed cruising speed of 120 MPH
AM radio station WOWO, Indiana's transmitter burns down
1st fireworks are held at Cleveland Stadium
1st trailside museum opens in Cleveland Metroparks
Work begins on Oakland Bay Bridge
Jordanians revolt in Amsterdam after reduction in employment
League of Nations ends sanctions against Italy after Italian takeover of Abyssinia
France-Turkish friendship treaty
Phillies complete move to Shribe Park in Philadelphia, lose 10-5 to Boston Bees
Red Sox 3rd baseman Jim Tabor hits record-tying 2 grand slams in one MLB game, 18-12 win v Phillies

WWII: British Navy bombards neutral French battle fleet at Mers El Kébir, near Oran, French Algeria to prevent Axis powers from taking the ships; 1267 French serviceman die, one ship sunk and 5 more damaged
Latvian partisans shoot 416 Jews dead
Massacre of the Lwów professors: Nazi occupying forces shoot and kill 25 Polish professors from Lwów and other family members
Politburo of Yugoslav communist party reorganizes
1st American bombing missions over Nazi Germany-occupied Europe (WWII)
US air offensive against Nazi Germany begins
1,100 US guns fire 4th of July salute at German lines in Normandy
Allied assault on Carpiquet airport at Caen
Gestapo arrests German Social Democrat Julius Leber
Field Marshal Henry Maitland Wilson gives Britain's agreement to use the atomic bomb against Japan at the Combined Policy Committee in Washington D. C.
Anti Jewish riots in Kielce Poland, 42 die
Philippines gains independence from US
Boston Braves slugger Sid Gordon ties MLB season grand slam record (4) with a bases loaded hit vs Phillies
Harry Truman signs public law 600 (Puerto Ricans write own constitution)
The first broadcast by Radio Free Europe.
Canadian Currency, Mint and Exchange Fund Act allows gold coins of $5, $10, and $20 to be minted
Imre Nagy succeeds Matyas Rákosi as premier of Hungary
Dr Sam Sheppard's wife Marilyn is murdered (he is accused of the crime)
FIFA World Cup Final, Wankdorf Stadium, Bern, Switzerland: Helmut Rahn scores twice as West Germany beats Hungary, 3-2
Meat and all other food rationing officially ends in Britain, nine years after the end of World War II
WMSL (WYUR, now WAFF) TV channel 48 in Huntsville, AL (ABC) begins
Independence National Historical Park forms in Philadelphia
US most intense rain fall (1.23" in 1 minute) at Unionville Maryland
Dutch 2nd Chamber accepts temporary tax increase
America's new 49-star flag honoring Alaska statehood unfurled
Cayman Islands separated from Jamaica, made a crown colony
Island Records founded in Jamaica
America's new 50-star flag honoring Hawaiian statehood unfurled
Walt Disney is one of the two main speakers on the Independence Day in The Rebuild Hills at Skørping in Denmark
KIKU (now KHNL) TV channel 13 in Honolulu, HI (IND) 1st broadcast
Beachboys' "I Get Around" reaches #1
Opening ceremony of Tassajara Zen Mountain Center
Radio astronomy satellite Explorer 38 launched
"Give Peace a Chance" single by John  Lennon & Plastic Ono Band is released in UK
Severe wind event, the Ohio Fireworks Derecho kills 18 people in Ohio and destroys over 100 boats on Lake Erie
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
100 injured in race rioting in Asbury Park, NJ
Chartered Dan-Air Comet crashes into mountains north of Barcelona, Spain killing 112 vacationing Britons
The Falls Road curfew in North Ireland, imposed by the British Army while searching for IRA weapons, continues throughout the day; a man is killed by the British Army
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
The Royal Ulster Constabulary forward a file about the killings on 'Bloody Sunday' (30 January 1972) to the Director of Public Prosecutions for Northern Ireland
CARICOM - Caribbean Community & Common Market, forms
Mike Marshall goes 9-0 with 3 saves in 20 appearances in 30 days
Opening ceremony of the Dai Bosatsu Zendo Kongo-ji Zen monastery at Livingston Manor, high in the Catskill Mountains of New York
Nigel Harrison replaces Gary Valentine as bassist of Blondie
Norman Lear's late night television talk show parody "Fernwood 2-Night", starring Martin Mull, Fred Willard, and Frank De Vol, debuts in syndication
Red Sox end 9-game losing streak by smashing a MLB-record 8 HRs in a 9-6 win vs Toronto at Fenway Park
Memphis fire fighters halt 3-day strike under a court order
4th NASA Space Shuttle Mission: Columbia 4 (STS-4) lands at Edwards AFB
Miguel de la Madrid Hurtado elected president of Mexico
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Discovery moves to Launch Pad 39B for STS-26 mission
US Navy shoots down Iranian civilian jetliner over Gulf, kills 290
Unmanned Russian Mig-23 crashes in Bellegem-Kooigem, Belgium (1 dies)
2 Live Crew release "Banned in the USA" the lyrics quote Star Spangled Banner and Gettysburg Address
400 New Kids on the Block fans treated for heat exhaustion in Minnesota
Battle of Hattin: Muslim armies underSaladindefeat Crusader forces near Tiberias in present-day Israel
Christian IIIis elected King of Denmark and Norway in the town of Rye
Battle of Klushino: KingSigismund III's Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth army defeats Russia and Sweden
States of Holland declare the "Eternal Edict" void and appointWilliam IIIas stadholder
Battle of Holowczyn: Swedish KingCharles XIIdefeats superior Russian force in surprising vctory
George Washingtongives Fort Necessity to France
William Pettybecomes Prime Minister of Great Britain following the death ofCharles Watson-Wentworth
GeologistJames Huttonpublicly reads an abstract of his theory of uniformitarianism for the first time at a meeting of the Royal Society of Edinburgh
The Louisiana Purchase is announced to the American people by PresidentThomas Jefferson
William Herschelmakes last telescopic observation of 1819 comet
American philosopher and naturalistHenry David Thoreaumoves into his shack on Walden Pond
In Brooklyn, New York, the first edition ofWalt Whitman's book of poems, "Leaves of Grass" is published
Charles Dodgson (Lewis Carroll) creates Alice in Wonderland for Alice Liddell during a family boat trip on the River Isis (Thames) in Oxford
General Lee's army withdraws from Gettysburg
Booker T. Washingtonestablishes Tuskegee Institute (Alabama)
Future founder of Pakistan, Muhammad AliJinnah, begins his studies at Sindh Madressatul Islam University in Karachi
Former US Federal judgeWilliam Howard Taftis installed as the 1st Governor-General of the Philippines, declares an amnesty for all insurgents who take an oath of allegiance
Civil government is established in the Philippines by a proclamation from US PresidentTheodore Roosevelt, who offers a general amnesty to insurgents
Baseball Hall of Fame pitchers Rube Waddell (A's) andCy Young(Boston) matchup in 20-inning classic; Philadelphia win, 4-2
Canadian world heavyweight boxing championTommy BurnsKOs Bill Squires of Australia in round 1 in Colma, California, his 6th title defense
"Fight of the Century":Jack JohnsonbeatsJames J. Jeffriesby TKO in 15 in Reno, Nevada to retain his world heavyweight boxing title
Chicago White Sox pitcherEd WalshhaltsTy Cobb's 40-game hitting streak as Cobb goes 0 for 4 in a 7-3 win over Detroit Tigers at Bennett Park
Jack Dempseybeats champion Jess Willard, retired in 3rd round in Toledo, Ohio for world heavyweight championship
Sukarnoand friends form the pro-Indonesian independence party, the PNI (Perserikatan Nasional Indonesia) in Batavia, Dutch East Indies
Don Bradmanscores a North American record 260 v Western Ontario in Guelph during Australian cricket team's Goodwill Tour of North America
Hungarian physicistLeó Szilárdpatents the chain-reaction design for the atomic bomb
Lou Gehrigis the first MLB player to have his number (4) retired on his "Appreciation Day" at Yankee Stadium and makes the iconic "luckiest man" speech
Howard Floreyand Norman Heatley meet for the first time and successfully recreate penicillin 11 days later
Irving Berlin's musical benefit "This Is The Army" , featuring a cast of US soldiers, opens at the Broadway Theatre, NYC; planned limited run of 4 weeks extended to twelve weeks
Siege of Sevastopolends with the surrender of Soviet forces and after massive German bombing raids that leave just 11 city buildings undamaged
PresidentManuel Roxasinaugurated as the 5th President of the Philippines and the 1st President of an independent Philippines at the Independence Grandstand, Manila
Yankees outfielderMickey Mantlehits 3-run homer vs Washington, 18th MLB player to hit 300 HRs
LBJsigns Freedom of Information Act
The Beatles are attacked by an angry mob in the Philippines after unintentionally insulting First LadyImelda Marcosby declining a state breakfast invitation
Arthur Kopit's play "Indians" based on the life of Buffalo Bill Cody premieres in London with the Royal Shakespeare Company

140,000 attend 2-day Atlanta Pop Festival in Byron, Georgia; performers include:Janis Joplin; Blood, Sweat & Tears; Chicago; Led Zeppelin; Delaney and Bonnie;Creedence Clearwater Revival, Canned Heat;Joe Cocker;Chuck Berry; Spirit; and Johnny Winter
Casey Kasem's countdown radio program "American Top 40" debuts on Los Angeles radio
Alan Ayckbourn's play "Absurd Person Singular," makes its London debut
Ted Bundyvictim Nancy Baird disappears from Layton, Utah
Algerian ex-presidentAhmed Ben Bellais freed after spending over a decade under house arrest
Nolan Ryan(Houston Astros) fans Reds' Cesar Geronimo to become 4th pitcher to 3,000 MLB strikeouts
NY Yankee pitcherDave Righettino-hits the Boston Red Sox, 4-0 at Yankee Stadium
New York Yankees pitcherPhil Niekrostrikes out Larry Parish (Texas Rangers) to become the 9th player to reach the 3,000 MLB strikeout milestone
NaziKlaus Barbie, "Butcher of Lyon" sentenced to life imprisonment in France
After a poor start to the season Kansas City Royals releaseDan Quisenberry(record 5-time AL saves leader)
14-year-old actressDrew Barrymore, attempts suicide
According to popular legend the Liberty Bell rings for the Second Continental Congress
US Congress proclaims the Declaration of Independence and independence from Great Britain
Past presidentsThomas Jeffersonand John Adams both die on the 50th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence, President John Quincy Adams calls "visible and palpable remarks of Divine Favor"
Statue of Liberty presented to US in Paris
Troops of the Russian Provisional Government open fire on protesters in Petrograd during the 'July Days' of unrest
The last Ottoman Sultan ascends the throne, Sultan Mehmed VI, who rules until the monarchy is abolished in 1922
1st Japanese kamikaze attack, US fleet near Iwo Jima
Ashikaga Yoshiakira, 2nd Japanese shōgun of the Ashikaga shogunate (1358-67), born in Japan (d. 1367)
Murad III, Ottoman Sultan (1574-95), born in Manisa, Ottoman Empire (d. 1595)
Cornelis Evertsen the Elder, Dutch-Swiss vice-admiral, born in Flushing, Netherlands (d. 1666)
Paul Scarron, French writer (Virgile Travesti), born in Paris (d. 1660)
Alexander von Papenhoven, Flemish religious sculptor, born in Antwerp (d. 1759)
Louis-Claude Daquin [d'Acquin], French organist and composer (La Rose), born in Paris (d. 1772)
Christian Fürchtegott Gellert, German poet and novelist (Fables & Tales), born in Hainchen, Germany (d. 1769)
Jean-Pierre Blanchard, French ballonist (1st balloon flights in England and U.S.), born in Les Andelys, France (d. 1809)
William Rush, American sculptor (Spirit of the Schuylkill), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1833)
Marco Santucci, Italian maestro di cappella (S. Giovanni (Laterano), 1797–1808), and composer, born in Camaiore, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1843)
Louis-Luc Loiseau de Persuis, French composer, born in Metz, France (d. 1819)
George Everest, Welsh surveyor and namesake of Mt. Everest, born in Gwernvale, Brecknockshire, Wales (d. 1866)
Franz Pecháček, Austrian-German composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1840)
Oscar I [Joseph François Oscar Bernadotte], French-Swedish noble, through adoption (King of Sweden and Norway, 1844-59), born in Paris, France (d. 1859)
Désiré de Haerne, Belgian Catholic priest and Congressional leader, born in Ypres, Belgium (d. 1890)
Hiram Walker, American entrepreneur and founder of the Hiram Walker and Sons Ltd. distillery, born in East Douglas, Massachusetts (d. 1899)
Reuben Fenton, American merchant and politician (22nd Governor of New York), born in Carroll, New York (d. 1885)
Green Clay Smith, American politician and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Richmond, Kentucky (d. 1895)
J. Johnston Pettigrew, American lawyer and Confederate General in the American Civil War, born in Tyrrell County, North Carolina (d. 1863)
Wilhelm Ahlwardt, German orientalist, born in Greifswald, Germany (d. 1909)
Hermann Kotzschmar, German-American composer, born in Finsterwalde, Brandenburg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1909)
James McGranahan, American composer, born in Adamsville, Pennsylvania (d. 1907)
Edward Gailliard, Flemish linguist and archaeologist, born in Bruges, Belgium (d. 1922)
Gustav Ratzenhofer, Austrian sociologist and general, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1904)
Gyula Erkel, Hungarian composer, son of Ferenc Erkel, born in Pest, Hungary (d. 1909)
Thomas John Barnardo, Irish-born British social worker, and philanthropist  (established Barnardo homes for children), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1905)
Ole Olsen, Norwegian composer, organist, and military bandleader, born in Hammerfest, Finnmark, Norway (d. 1927)
Heinrich Zöllner, German composer, born in Leipzig, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1941)
Victor Babeş, Romanian physician and bacteriologist, born in Vienna, Austrian Empire (d. 1926)
Mickey Welch, American Baseball HOF pitcher (third to 300 career wins; Troy Trojans, New York Giants), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1941)
Stephen Mather, American industrialist and conservationist (organized US National Park Service), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1930)
Henrietta Swan Leavitt, American astronomer (period-luminosity relation), born in Lancaster, Massachusetts (d. 1921)
Pieter van der Lijn, Dutch geologist (Dutch boulders), born in Graft, Netherlands (d. 1964)
David Denton, English cricket batsman (11 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 104; Yorkshire CCC), born in Wakefield, England (d. 1950)
Giovanni & Giacomo Tocci, Italian con-joined twins, born in Locana (probable date)
William Farnum, American actor (Spoilers, Man who Fights Alone), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1953)
George Mullin, American baseball pitcher (Detroit Tigers; no-hitter 1912), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 1944)
Pat Rooney, American vaudevillian and actor (Night Club), born in New York City (d. 1962)
Ulysses S. Grant III, American soldier and planner, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1968)
Maximilian Steinberg, Russian composer, born in Vilnius, Russian Empire (d. 1946)
Alexander Byvanck, Dutch art historian and archaeologist, born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1970)

Lucy Diggs Slowe, American educator (Dean of Women, Howard University, 1922-37), tennis champion (American Tennis Association, 1917), and sorority co-founder (Alpha Kappa Alpha, 1908), born in Berryville, Virginia (d. 1937)
Heinrich Kaminski, German composer, born in Tiengen, Schwarzwald, Germany (d. 1946)
Henry Armetta, Italian-American actor (The Black Cat, Bell for Adano, Big Store), born in Palermo, Sicily, Italy (d. 1945)
Henry "Doc" Carlson, American Basketball Hall of Fame collegiate coach (University of Pittsburgh, 1922-53: 367–248 [.597]), originated figure 8 play, born in Murray City, Ohio (d. 1964)
Eric Marx, South African cricket all-rounder (3 Tests, 3 wickets; 240 on FC debut for Transvaal), born in Johannesburg, South Africa (d. 1974)
Irving Caesar, American lyricist and theater composer ("Tea for Two"; "Just A Gigilo"; "Sometimes I'm Happy"; "Animal Crackers In My Soup"), born in New York City (d. 1996)[1]
Massimo Campigli, Italian painter and illustrator, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1971)
Adolph Hallis, South African concert pianist, classical and film score composer, and teacher, born in Port Elizabeth, South Africa (d. 1987)
Mao Dun [Shen Dehong], Chinese writer (Midnight), born in Tongxiang, China (d. 1981)
Gulzarilal Nanda, Indian politician and economist, temporary Prime Minister of India (1964, 1966), born in Sialkot, British India (d. 1998)
Johnny Lee, American actor (Song of the South, The Amos 'n' Andy Show), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1965)
Pilar Barbosa, Puerto Rican historian and political activist, born in Bayamon, Puerto Rico (d. 1997)
Vince Barnett, American actor (Star is Born, Human Jungle), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1977)
Flor Peeters, Flemish organist (St. Rumbold's Cathedral - Mechelen, 1923-86), composer (Lied Symphony), and pedagogue, born in Tielen, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1986)
Rudolf Breslauer, German Jewish photographer (documented Westerbork transit camp), born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 1945)
Angela Baddeley, English actress (Ghost Train, Tom Jones), born in London, England (d. 1976)
Artur Malawski, Polish violinist, conductor, composer (Mountaineers Triptych), and pedagogue, born in Prömsel, Galicia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1957)
Isaac Bashevis Singer, Polish-American Yiddish writer (Yentl, Nobel Prize for Literature 1978), born in Leoncin, Russian Empire (d. 1991)
Seger Ellis, American jazz pianist, pop vocalist, songwriter, and bandleader, born in Houston, Texas (d. 1995)[1]
Dante Fiorillo, American composer, born in New York City (d. 1970)
Irving Johnson, American sail training pioneer and adventurer, born in Hadley, Massachusetts (d. 1991)
Lionel Trilling, American literary critic and author (1969 Poses Award, The Liberal Imagination), born in New York City (d. 1975)
Vincent Joseph Schaefer, American chemist and meteorologist (cloud seeding), born in Schenectady, New York (d. 1993)
Elliott Sullivan, American actor (The Persuaders!, Fury Below, Sergeant), born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 1974)

Gordon Griffith, American silent and sound screen child actor, first to portray Tarzan on film (as Young Tarzan in "Tarzan of the Apes" (1918)), assistant director, and film producer, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1958)
Howard Taubman, American music and theater critic, born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1996)
Aurelio Peccei, Italian industrialist and chairman (Club of Rome), born in Turin, Piedmont, Italy (d. 1984)
Alec Templeton, Welsh pianist and composer (Concertino Lirico), born in Cardiff, Wales (d. 1963)
Lynette Roberts, British poet, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1995)
Madeleine Barot, French activist and theologian, born in Châteauroux, France (d. 1995)

(William Thomas) "Champion Jack" Dupree, American boxer, blues and boogie-woogie pianist, singer-songwriter ("Walkin' The Blues"), and WWII P.O.W., born in Irish Channel, New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1992)
Francis D. Foley, US Rear Admiral (WW II, Guadalcanal), born in Dorchester, Massachusetts (d. 1999)
Robert K. Merton, American sociologist (functionalism, self-fulfilling prophecy) and criminologist, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2003)
Bikram Singh, Indian Army Lt. General (Kashmir), born in British India (d. 1963)
Mitch Miller, American oboist and recording producer (Sing Along with Mitch), born in Rochester, New York (d. 2010)
Virginia Graham, American TV personality (Girl Talk, Where Was I), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1998)
Viviane Romance [Pauline Ronacher Ortmanns], French actress (Panic, Queen's Necklace), born in Roubaix, Nord, France (d. 1991)
Nuccio Bertone, Italian automobile designer, born in Turin, Piedmont, Italy (d. 1997)
Christine Lavant, Austrian poet and writer (Das Kind), born in Lavant Valley, Carinthia, Austria (d. 1973)
Timmie Rogers, American comedian (The Jackie Gleason Show), actor and singer-songwriter ("Back to School Again"; "I Love Ya, I Love Ya, I Love Ya"), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2006)
Fernand Leduc, Canadian abstract expressionist painter (Les Automatistes), and teacher, born in Viauville, Montreal, Quebec (d. 2014)
Tokyo Rose [Iva Toguri D'Aquino], American-born Japanese propagandist (WWII), born in Los Angeles (d. 2006)
Manolete [Manuel Laureano Rodríguez Sánchez], Spanish bullfighter, born in Córdoba, Andalucia, Spain (d. 1947)

Abigail Van Buren [Pauline Phillips], American columnist and radio show host known for "Dear Abby" advice column, twin sister of fellow advice columnist Ann Landers (Eppie Lederer), born in Sioux City, Iowa (d. 2013)
Ann Landers [Eppie Lederer], American advice columnist "Ask Ann Landers," twin sister of fellow advice columnist "Dear Abby" (Abigail Van Buren), born in Sioux City, Iowa (d. 2002)
Buster Davis, American choral director (Garry Moore Show), born in Johnstown, Pennsylvania (d. 1987)
Johnnie Parsons, American auto racer (Indianapolis 500, 1950; AAA/USAC Championship, 1949), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1984)
Tāufaʻāhau Tupou IV, King of Tonga (1965-2006), born in Royal Palace, Nuku'alofa, Tonga (d. 2006)
Leona Helmsley, American real estate billionaire convicted of federal income tax evasion, born in Marbletown, New York (d. 2007)
Norm Drucker, American basketball referee (NBA Supervisor of Officials 1977–81; ABA Supervisor of the Officials 1969–73), born in New York City (d. 2015)
Gérard Debreu, French-American economist (Nobel Prize, 1983), born in Calais, France (d. 2004)
Tibor Varga, Hungarian violinist and orchestra leader (Ripley's Game), born in Győr, Hungary (d. 2003)
Ghulam Ahmed, Indian cricket spin bowler (22 Tests, 68 wickets, BB 7/49, 1 x 50; Hyderabad), born in Hyderabad, India (d. 1998)
Rudolf Friedrich, Swiss politician and member of the Swiss Federal Council (1982-84), born in Winterthur, Switzerland (d. 2013)
Eva Marie Saint, American actress and producer (On the Waterfront, North by Northwest, People Like Us), born in Newark, New Jersey

Harry Stewart Jr., American US Army Air Forces fighter pilot and officer (Tuskegee Airmen, WWII), instructor, and co-winner of the first 'Top Gun" competition (1949), born in Newport News, Virginia (d. 2025)[1]
Mary Stuart, American actress (Jo-Search for Tomorrow), born in Miami, Florida (d. 2002)
Luigina "Gina" Lollobrigida, Italian actress (Trapeze; Falcon Crest), photojournalist, and sculptor, born in Subiaco, Italy (d. 2023)[1]
Reid Miles, American photographer, and album cover art designer (Blue Note Records, 1955-67), and Clio Award-winning TV commercial director, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1993)
Cathy Berberian, American mezzo-soprano and composer (Stripsody), born in Attleboro, Massachusetts (d. 1983)
Giampiero Boniperti, Italian soccer striker (38 caps; Juventus 443 games), executive (chairman Juventus) and politician (deputy in European Parliament), born in Barengo, Italy (d. 2021)
Patrick Tilley, British sci-fi author (The Amtrak Wars: First Family, Blood River), born in Southend-on-Sea, England (d. 2020)
Paul de Wispelaere, Flemish writer and critic, born in Bruges, Belgium (d. 2016)
Sylvius Gerald Marie Rozendal, Premier of Netherlands Antilles, born in Willemstad, Curacao (d. 2003)
Chuck Tanner, American baseball manager (World Series 1979 Pittsburgh Pirates; Chicago WS, Oakland A's, Atlanta Braves), born in New Castle, Pennsylvania (d. 2011)
Peter Angelos, American lawyer and baseball executive (majority owner of the Baltimore Orioles, 1993-2024), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2024)[1]
Frunzik [Mher Mkrtchyan], Armenian actor (Mimino, Kidnapping, Caucasian Style), born in Leninakan, Soviet Armenia (d. 1993)
Yury Tyukalov, Soviet rower (Olympic 1952,56 Gold), born in Leningrad, Russia (d. 2018)
